text: Cognitive psychologists use the term "metacognitive illusion" to describe the systematic gap between perceived and actual comprehension. In a classic demonstration, Reder and Ritter showed that people reliably predict they understand a text after reading it once, yet fail retrieval tests moments later. The illusion arises because fluency — the ease with which text is processed — is mistaken for depth of encoding. Familiar vocabulary and coherent prose feel like comprehension even when nothing durable is being retained. This is why re-reading produces far less learning than retrieval practice: passive re-exposure reinforces the feeling of knowing without building the structure of knowledge.
question: According to the passage, what creates the metacognitive illusion of comprehension?

options.text: The tendency to skim rather than read carefully
Fluency being mistaken for depth of encoding
Forgetting that occurs between reading and testing
